reels.caption: 🇧🇷 Alex Pereira questionou os golpes recebidos contra Cyril Gane e criticou a atuação de Herb Dean. Já o árbitro respond
good: Hook is scroll-stopping: creator's face fills frame with bold, high-contrast text posing a controversy question in Portuguese—directly addresses the target audience
Strong narrative arc: question → creator reaction → fight footage → referee explanation creates intrigue loop that drives shares
Creator branding visible (PEACE GRAPPLER shirt) builds trust and channel identity
Bilingual captions expand reach to Portuguese and English speakers; controversy angle (arbitration dispute) naturally encourages discussion
bad: Watch time drops mid-video (11.6s average suggests viewers bail before the end)—transition from exciting fight footage to static referee footage loses momentum
Low saves (14) despite decent engagement indicates low perceived value for bookmarking or return viewing—content is consumable but not clip-worthy
No visible call-to-action: missing 'tag someone,' 'comment your take,' or 'share this' to unlock viral potential of controversy
Frames 3–4 are visually flat and lower-energy—referee talking head and training footage lack the impact of the hook and early fight action
reels.top_tip: End with the most controversial/dramatic fight moment (not the referee explanation) to prevent watch-time dropout; pair it with a direct CTA like 'tag someone who agrees' to convert the controversy into shares

reels.caption:
🇧🇷 Topuria realmente errou ou o Gaethje foi o responsável por criar todos os problemas dentro do octógono? 👀🔥
Nesse trec
good: Hook uses debate format ("EGO OU ERRO?") to create curiosity — drives strong watch-time (9.3s avg) and engagement rate (20.7%)
Credibility signals via fighter names, UFC branding, official logos — builds trust and justifies viewer attention
High share rate (185 shares on 5.6k views) — debate format resonates and spreads
Clear YouTube CTA at end funnels engaged viewers to full content
bad: Saves nearly non-existent (2 out of 5,667 views) — debate lacks memorable takeaway viewers would bookmark
Hook relies on text graphic + talking head instead of dynamic fight footage — less visually arresting than typical MMA scroll-stoppers
Production quality gap between talking-head segments and professional UFC octagon footage — inconsistent polish
reels.top_tip: Replace the text-heavy hook with 2-3 seconds of actual Topuria vs Gaethje fight footage showing the contested moment — fight clips stop scrolls better than graphics AND create a memorable visual anchor that viewers will save as reference

reels.caption: 🇺🇸 Ngannou made more in one boxing fight than his UFC career. McGregor lost to Floyd and still made around $100 million.
good: Hook typography is scroll-stopping: high-contrast red 'BOXING PAYS BETTER' text on dark background immediately communicates the premise
$100M check is a powerful visual anchor—instantly recognizable symbol of financial disparity that drives emotional engagement
Strong retention: 28.9s average watch time indicates pacing and frame transitions keep viewers watching through the narrative
Clear problem-solution structure: hook establishes intrigue, check provides payoff, frames reinforce the comparison
bad: Share rate severely underperforms (15 shares from 1,282 views = 1.2%) despite scroll-stopping hook—content isn't prompting organic amplification
No visible call-to-action: missing 'Tag someone', 'Follow', or 'Share' prompt to convert attention into virality
Fighter identity ambiguity for casual viewers—frame 3's small images don't clearly communicate these are the same fighters unless audience knows UFC/boxing
Negligible saves (2) despite 24% engagement—content entertains but doesn't feel reference-worthy or bookmark-able
reels.top_tip: Add explicit on-screen text in frame 2 connecting identity ('McGregor vs. Ngannou: Same fighter, $100M difference') + a CTA overlay ('Tag someone who should switch sports') to convert your strong hook and visuals into share-driven growth—you're leaving 80% of amplification potential untapped

reels.caption:
🇺🇸 Conor McGregor and Max Holloway are already fighting long before July.
The punches haven't started yet.The mind games
good: Bilingual captions (English/Portuguese) expand addressable audience
Strong engagement rate of 74.3% shows content resonates with viewers who see it
High share count (58) indicates audience investment in the conflict narrative
Authentic dialogue/quotes capture MMA trash-talk authenticity that drives shares
Visual variety across frames (different angles, physique, hat detail) maintains interest
bad: Only 660 views suggests hook isn't stopping cold scrollers effectively
Minimal saves (2) despite high engagement—content feels disposable, not reference-worthy
Text overlays are primary content rather than accompanying dynamic fight footage typical of MMA reels
White text on dark faces lacks contrast; harder to read while scrolling at speed
No explicit call-to-action (e.g., 'tag who wins', 'comment your pick')—engagement is passive
reels.top_tip: Replace static face close-ups in the hook with actual fight footage or higher-contrast visuals with 2-3x larger text—660 views means the algorithm isn't prioritizing this for cold reach despite 74.3% engagement; the first 0.5s needs more scroll-stopping visual pop

reels.caption: 🇧🇷 Mais um card com o Brasil representado no UFC. Alguns nomes buscam subir no ranking, outros tentam manter o momento.
good: Instant visual hook: shirtless fighters sparring in gym immediately stops scroll for combat sports audience
Crystal-clear fighter identification: large text overlays with names and date (06.20) prevent viewer confusion
Exceptional engagement rate (43.4%): direct prediction question in caption ('Who will you see winning?') drives comments and engagement well above platform average
Strong bilingual reach: Portuguese + English caption targets both Brazilian fighters' core audience and international UFC fans
bad: Severe save-to-share imbalance: 33 shares vs 2 saves—content designed as 'send to friend' not 'save for later,' missing algorithm opportunity
Training gym footage lacks cinematic production: feels behind-the-scenes rather than championship-level, reducing perceived prestige
Weak matchup framing: caption asks 'who wins' but video doesn't show direct head-to-head visual comparison to anchor predictions
reels.top_tip: Add end-card 'Save for June 20' text with date graphic—engagement is strong and shares are great, but convert that audience to saves with a time-sensitive 'bookmark this card' CTA to boost algorithmic reach

reels.caption: 🇧🇷 Cutelaba aposta na pressão e no perigo dos primeiros minutos. Stirling chega invicto, confiando na técnica e na movim
good: Hook frame uses bold high-contrast text ('CUTELABA VS STIRLING') with red/white overlay that stops scrolls immediately
Clear narrative arc (underestimation → skill display → opponent fading → prediction) delivers a complete story in 7.6s
Real fight footage maintains tension and authenticity; expert reaction at end adds credibility
Strong engagement rate (62.7%) and solid average watch time shows viewers are hooked
bad: Only 1 save from 776 views — content is entertaining but not valuable enough to bookmark for later
24 shares (3.1% of views) is very low — no clear reason or prompt for viewers to share their take
Missing viewer participation element — no question overlay, poll, or call-to-action to convert passive watches into comments or tags
Stirling (the opponent) is barely featured despite equal billing in the title — unbalanced storytelling
reels.top_tip: Add a direct question CTA on the final frame ('Who do YOU think wins?' or 'Stirling or Cutelaba?') to convert high watch time into comments and shares — fight content thrives on prediction engagement, not passive consumption

reels.caption: 🇧🇷 No UFC, um golpe pode mudar tudo. Kape, Stirling, Vinicius Oliveira, Chokheli e outros mostraram mais uma vez por que
good: Strong hook text overlay—'ONE HORIELIC KNOCKOUT' in bold white creates immediate contrast and signals impact moment
High engagement rate (39.4%) and solid average watch time (6.5s) indicate the hook successfully stops scrolls
Bilingual caption targets both Portuguese and English audiences, expanding reach potential
Real UFC footage adds authenticity and credibility that resonates with combat sports audience
bad: Black opening frame with only logo (0.5s)—zero visual hook before action; risks immediate scroll-past; should open with striking moment or fighter's face
Blurred action in early frames—excitement isn't crystal clear; viewers need crisp, high-contrast visuals in first 1-3 seconds

Very low save count (1) and share count (2) relative to 553 views—content isn't sticky enough to drive the 'save to watch later' or 'share with friends' actions; needs clearer emotional payoff or hype moment
reels.top_tip: Replace the black logo opening with a crisp, well-lit highlight of the most violent knockout (impact moment or fighter's face reaction) appearing within 0.5s. Overlay 'ONE STRIKE, EVERYTHING CHANGES' or similar text immediately to compound the hook. This removes dead space and leads with visual impact, which will improve scroll-stop rate and likely increase overall views.

reels.caption: 🇧🇷 Um traz poder explosivo. O outro traz experiência e uma vitória no confronto direto. Uma disputa de cinturão pode est
good: Hook effectiveness: Fighter faces dominate first frame with high-contrast text overlay — immediate scroll-stop visual
Strong watch retention: 6.1s average watch time suggests narrative pacing (prediction build-up) keeps viewers through key moments
Bilingual captions (PT/EN) expand geographic reach without production overhead
High engagement rate (44.1%) indicates the hook and production quality are resonating with core audience
bad: Zero shares despite decent views — prediction/analysis format lacks viral 'had to show my friend' factor
Critically low save rate (1) — viewers aren't returning for this content; not positioning as a reference or highlight worth keeping
Missing call-to-action — no prompt to comment predictions, debate outcome, or tag a friend
Limited reach (460) relative to views (698) — narrow algorithmic amplification suggests content isn't breaking out of niche audience
reels.top_tip: Add an explicit CTA at the end (e.g., 'Who takes it?' or 'Comment your prediction') to convert passive viewers into commenters — retention is strong, but engagement funnels to zero because there's no prompt beyond watching
